text: An endoscopic view shows the lumen of the bowel, where a small tube is inserted to aid in decompression.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection of the colon, carefully separating tissue layers to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the dense, immobile tissue in the pelvis, indicative of severe adhesion or fibrosis.
The surgeon observes a proximal loop of the sigmoid colon that is adhered over the pelvic structures, indicating dense adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon targets the superior hemorrhoidal artery, preparing to dissect or ligate it within the pelvic region.
The surgeon meticulously cleans the vascular adventitia to isolate the vessel, revealing the distinct structure of the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the bifurcation where the left colic artery branches off, visible near the surgeon's left hand.
The surgeon identifies the superior hemorrhoidal artery, located just distal to the left colic artery.
The surgeon prepares to take the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, which has been isolated by cleaning off the vascular adventitia.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection on the left colon, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon navigates down towards the pelvis, encountering dense adhesions on the left pelvic sidewall during the dissection.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, aiming to connect it with the previously dissected medial space, while ensuring the ureter remains posterior to the sponge.
The surgeon exposes the left pelvic brim, revealing dense fibrotic tissue and adhesions in the area.
The surgeon identifies the ureter on the left pelvic sidewall, amidst dense fibrotic tissue and adhesions.
The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to check the ureter's location, with a sponge placed on top of the ureter for protection, while catheters are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the location of the sponge within the surgical field, positioned near the ureter on the left pelvic brim amidst fibrotic and dense adherence.
The surgeon begins to mobilize the colon out of the pelvis after completing the dissection at the splenic flexure.
The surgeon uses scissors for dissection while holding a suction device in the left hand to clear smoke, blood, debris, and pus from the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates down the left pelvic sidewall, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate and expose the area during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully navigates through dense tissue using scissors, with the ureter visible nearby, ensuring it is not inadvertently drawn into the dissection area.
The surgeon manipulates the loop of sigmoid colon that has descended into the pelvis, carefully positioning instruments to hug the serosa of the colon.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, navigating through the plane that shows resistance to fibrotic reaction.
The video shows the loop of sigmoid colon adhered to the top of the rectum, with the serosa visible.
The surgeon exposes the true rectum, revealing its anatomical structure during the dissection process.
The surgeon moves to the right-hand side of the surgical field, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon marks the area on the rectum where the transection is planned, using visual cues to identify the appropriate site.
The surgeon manipulates the loop of sigmoid colon that was previously flapped over, repositioning it back into the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane on the left-hand side.
The surgeon encounters a dense mass of cemented pericolic fat and epiploic appendages, appearing as rock-hard fibrotic tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to work around the surgical field, maneuvering within the TME plane to address the sigmoid loop and surrounding fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon continues the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on the visible tissue planes and structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon successfully accesses the softer tissues of the rectal mesentery, maneuvering through the previously encountered fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to clear the rectal mesentery, preparing it for a trim section.
The surgeon prepares to staple the distal section of the rectum, following the dissection of the rectal mesentery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and remove the remaining portion of the rectal mesentery, completing the tissue separation in the area.
The surgeon meticulously cleans the rectal mesentery, ensuring it is completely free from any residual tissue to prevent it from getting caught in the stapler or causing bleeding.
The surgeon addresses the last section of the posterior mesentery, carefully dissecting it down to the rectal wall.
The surgeon prepares to staple the rectal wall after completing the dissection and cleaning of the mesentery.
The surgeon uses a 45mm stapler deep in the pelvis to secure the distal end of the rectal mesentery.
The surgeon places one final staple to complete the closure of the posterior mesentery onto the rectal wall, ensuring the area is clean to prevent mesentery entrapment or bleeding.
The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery while sparing the left colic artery to ensure adequate perfusion.
The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery, advancing towards the planned proximal margin of resection.
The surgeon checks perfusion with a stapler in place, then proceeds to transect the colon. The ends of the colon are kept closed to isolate the specimen, due to the remote chance of cancer presence.
The surgeon begins a hand-sewn anastomosis, visually connecting the ends of the colon with sutures.
The surgeon prepares to perform a hand-sewn anastomosis, avoiding the use of staplers due to the presence of two stapled ends of the colon.
The surgeon prepares to perform a hand-sewn anastomosis on the colon, with the proximal sigmoid visibly stretched and positioned in the pelvis.
The surgeon prepares to perform a hand-sewn anastomosis after creating staple lines on the bowel ends.
The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, visually aligning and securing the bowel ends with sutures.
The surgeon completes the posterior row of sutures in the two-layer anastomosis during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins performing a proctotomy, making an incision in the rectal tissue as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a collotomy, making an incision into the colon as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins suturing in the center of the anastomosis site, initially sewing towards the left side.
The surgeon begins suturing the canal, ensuring not to close it too much to maintain visibility of the back row, and then starts the next suture by sewing to the right.
The surgeon begins to canal over the top, continuing the surgical process after completing the back row.
The surgeon ties the inner anterior row sutures together for security, reinforcing the inner suture line during the anastomosis.
The surgeon takes seromuscular bites with the suture needle, focusing on the outer layers of the bowel wall during the anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully approximates the serosa over the top of the anastomosis, ensuring the outer layer is brought together without excessive tension.
The surgeon ties the sutures together to secure the anastomosis, reinforcing the inner anterior row.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the newly constructed anastomosis, checking for any visible signs of fluid leakage from the sutured area.
The surgeon uses a 19mm proctoscope during the procedure.
The surgeon observes the distension of the bowel, indicating successful inflation during the leak test.
The surgeon navigates through the anastomosis site, visually inspecting the area to ensure proper connection and integrity.
The surgeon clears out the irrigation fluid from the surgical field, marking the end of the procedure.
